1: bo el-Paroh: "Go to Pharaoh." The second plague cycle begins. The frog plague has Egyptian religious dimensions: frogs were associated with Heqet, the frog-headed goddess of fertility and midwifery. The plague inverts this: the emblem of fertility becomes an infestation of the most intimate spaces.
2: negof et-kol-gevulcha batsfardeiyim: "I will plague your whole country with frogs." Nagaf (H5062) — to strike, plague; related to nega (H5061, plague, stroke). The Hiphil participle of shalach and the noun makka (blow, plague, H4347) together are the plague narrative's vocabulary of divine striking. The frogs' reach is total: palace, bedroom, bed, kitchen, court.
3: uvemishtekha uvethanurekha: "your kneading bowls, your ovens." The invasion reaches the most intimate domestic spaces of food preparation. The inversion is complete: the Nile's fertility blessing, which made Egypt's agriculture possible, now desecrates the food supply itself. The frogs in the kneading bowls render food preparation impossible.
4: uvecha uvaamcha uvchol-avadecha: "over you, your people, and all your officials." The hierarchy addressed — king, people, officials — frames the plague as touching the complete social order. This is consistent across the plagues: the divine judgment is not class-specific but touches Egypt's entire social structure from the top down.
5: neteh et-yadcha bemateikha: "Stretch out your hand with your staff." The matteh (staff) and natah yad (stretch out the hand) form the plague-initiation formula. The same gesture initiates the sea crossing (Exodus 14:16,21,26,27), creating a structural link between the plague-hand and the sea-crossing-hand. YHWH's outstretched hand is the consistent instrument of both judgment and salvation.
6: vataal hatsfardeiyah: "Frogs came up (alah)." Alah (H5927) — to go up, ascend. The frogs "go up" from the Nile over the land — using the verb for ascent that will later describe Israel's "going up" out of Egypt (Exodus 13:18; Numbers 20:5). The frogs' ascending infestation is a counter-image to Israel's ascending departure.
7: vaya'asu khen hachartumaim belat'eihem: "The magicians did the same." The magicians replicate the plague by adding more frogs — but cannot remove the infestation they have added to. Their power extends to multiplication of the problem but not to its resolution. This limitation will be fully exposed at gnats (Exodus 8:18): they can bring life but cannot uncreate what YHWH has made.
8: hatiru el-YHWH: "Pray to the LORD." Athar (H6279) — to pray, entreat; used for intercessory prayer (Genesis 25:21; Numbers 21:7; Judges 13:8). Pharaoh's first request for intercession recognizes Moses's access to YHWH as something Pharaoh himself lacks. The intercessor who prays on behalf of the enemy who enslaved his people is the first of many such mediatorial paradoxes in Moses's career.
9: hitpa'er alai: "I leave to you the honor of setting the time." Literally "glorify yourself over me" — Moses offers Pharaoh the rhetorical advantage of choosing the schedule. The move is strategically significant: if Pharaoh sets the time and the frogs disappear precisely at that time, the timed fulfillment becomes an undeniable demonstration of YHWH's control. Moses is engineering an epistemological proof, not merely performing a service.
10: lemaan tedah ki-ein kYHWH Eloheinu: "so that you may know there is no one like the LORD our God." The incomparability formula — ein kamoni ("there is none like me," Exodus 9:14) / ein kYHWH ("none like the LORD") — is the theological claim the plagues are designed to demonstrate. Each plague is an argument; the argument is the uniqueness and incomparability of YHWH.
11: rak banehar tish'arna: "they will remain only in the Nile." The frogs' removal to the Nile — the source from which they came — is the reversal of their coming. The Nile, the focus of Egypt's worship and the origin of the plague, receives them back. The divine power that deploys from the Nile also retracts to the Nile.
12: vayitsa Mosheh... vayitsar: "Moses cried out (tsaaq, H6817) to the LORD." Tsaaq is the cry of distress and urgent appeal — the same verb used for Israel's cry under bondage (Exodus 2:23; Exodus 3:7,9). Moses uses the language of oppressed Israel in his prayer on behalf of oppressive Egypt. The intercession that uses the vocabulary of the victim's cry is a profound irony.
13: vaya'as YHWH kidvar Mosheh: "The LORD did what Moses asked." The compliance formula applied to YHWH's response to Moses's prayer mirrors the compliance formula applied to Moses's response to YHWH's commands (Exodus 7:6,10,20). YHWH and Moses are in a reciprocal relationship of command-and-compliance that defines the partnership of the plague narrative.
14: chamarim chamarim: "great heaps." Chomer (H2563) — heap, pile. The dead frogs piled into heaps recall the piled corpses of Pharaoh's army at the sea (Exodus 14:30). "The land reeked" (tivash haarets) — the same root bo'esh as the Nile's stench in Exodus 7:18,21. The stench of each plague gives way to the stench of the next; Egypt accumulates evidence of its refusal.
15: hakhbeid et-libbo velo shama: "he hardened his heart (kaved) and would not listen." Here kaved (H3515) is used with Pharaoh explicitly as the subject: "he made his heart heavy." The MT's reading places the hardening initiative squarely with Pharaoh at the moment of relief. The pattern is: crisis → temporary compliance → relief → self-hardening → refusal. Pharaoh hardens when the pressure is off.
16: hach et-afar haarets: "Strike the dust of the ground." The third plague uses afar (dust, H6083) — the material of the earth, specifically of dry land. The plagues have moved from water (Nile blood) to water-plus-earth (frogs from the Nile) to earth itself (dust to gnats). The no-warning feature (unlike plagues 1 and 2) is the defining characteristic of each triad's third plague.
17: kinnim (H3654): the precise insect is disputed: gnats, mosquitoes, or lice (LXX skniphes, Josephus phtheires/lice). The attack on the dust-of-the-ground reverses the creation of humanity from dust (Genesis 2:7): the dust that was the raw material of God's creation-blessing becomes the raw material of divine judgment on Egypt's civilization.
18: vaya'asu khen hachartumaim lehotsi et-hakinim veloh yakholu: "The magicians tried to produce gnats but they could not." The first absolute failure of Egyptian magic. The inability to produce gnats from dust — the very material of creation — marks the boundary of occult power. What YHWH makes from earth, no human art can replicate. The magicians' progressive failure: replicate (blood, Exodus 7:22), replicate (frogs, Exodus 8:7), fail (gnats, Exodus 8:18).
19: etsba Elohim hi: "This is the finger of God!" The magicians' theological interpretation is the pivot of the first six plagues. Etsba Elohim (H676 + H430) — finger of God. This is the same phrase Jesus uses in Luke 11:20 to interpret his exorcisms: "if it is by the finger of God that I cast out demons, then the kingdom of God has come upon you." The Exodus phrase becomes the christological phrase for the in-breaking of God's kingdom through Jesus's ministry.
20: hashkem baboker: "Get up early in the morning." The morning confrontation at the water (cf. Exodus 7:15) suggests YHWH knows Pharaoh's routine. The fourth plague (flies, arob) opens the second triad (flies, livestock, boils), which introduces the Goshen distinction — escalating the theological drama from demonstrated power to selective protection.
21: arob (H6157): swarms, literally "mixture" — the exact insect is debated (LXX kynomuia, dog-flies; Psalms 78:45 "swarms"). The comprehensive impact ("the houses of Egypt were ruined, and also the ground on which they stood") uses shachat (to ruin, corrupt, destroy) — the same verb as the flood narrative's "the earth was corrupt" (Genesis 6:11,12). The plague-vocabulary deliberately echoes the primeval destruction.
22: vehafleti et-erets Goshen: "I will set apart (pala) the land of Goshen." Pala (H6395, Hiphil) — to make wonderful, to distinguish, to treat as exceptional. The rare verb (also Exodus 9:4; Exodus 11:7) conveys that YHWH's protection of Goshen is not a natural phenomenon but a miraculous differentiation. "By this you will know that I, the LORD, am in the midst of this land" — YHWH's presence is argued from the geographic precision of his protection.
23: pedut (H6304): distinction, separation — possibly from padah (to redeem, H6299). If the root is redemptive, the "distinction" YHWH places between his people and Pharaoh's people is simultaneously a redemptive act. The ambiguity may be deliberate: the "distinction" in the plagues anticipates the "redemption" at the sea (Exodus 6:6: "I will redeem you with an outstretched arm").
24: kabed arob bayit Paroh: "Dense (kaved) swarms of flies poured into Pharaoh's palace." The plague itself is kaved (heavy) — the same adjective used of Pharaoh's heavy heart (Exodus 7:14; Exodus 8:15,32; Exodus 9:7). The heavy-hearted Pharaoh receives a heavy plague; the descriptor mirrors the disposition.
25: lechu zivchu lEloheichem baarets: "Go, sacrifice to your God here in the land." Pharaoh's first compromise: in-country sacrifice. Moses rejects it: the problem is not merely distance but the nature of the sacrifice. Sacrificing cattle (sacred to Egypt) in Egyptian territory would provoke Egyptians to violence; the three-day journey into the wilderness is specifically required.
26: to'avat Mitsrayim: "detestable to the Egyptians." To'evah (H8441) — abomination, detestable thing. The same word used throughout Leviticus and Deuteronomy for practices incompatible with Israel's covenantal identity. Here applied inversely: Israel's sacrifice is an abomination to Egypt. The clash of incompatible religious systems is the theological backdrop to the exodus's necessity.
27: derekh shloshes yamim: "three-day journey." The original demand (Exodus 3:18; Exodus 5:3) maintained throughout the negotiations. Each of Pharaoh's compromises attempts to reduce the scope of the departure — in-country sacrifice (Exodus 8:25), don't go too far (Exodus 8:28) — without meeting YHWH's actual requirement. The total and unconditional departure is non-negotiable.
28: rak harbek lo tirchiku laleches: "but you must not go very far." Pharaoh's second compromise imposes a distance restriction on a departure he is ostensibly granting. The attempt to limit the scope of Israel's service to YHWH is the structural equivalent of every partial obedience — the departure without the departure.
29: hoel Paroh hotoel: "let Pharaoh not continue to deceive (tal tal)." The verb tal (H8591) — to mock, deceive, make a fool of — is Moses's first explicit accusation of Pharaoh's bad faith. Despite accepting Pharaoh's terms, Moses recognizes and names the pattern: Pharaoh is playing for temporary relief, not intending genuine compliance. The accusation proves accurate (Exodus 8:32).
30: vayitaar el-YHWH: "prayed to the LORD." The intercessory movement — leave the human court, approach the divine court — is Moses's characteristic pattern throughout the plague narrative. His departure from Pharaoh's presence is the signal that intercession begins. The prayer's content is not recorded; the result speaks for it.
31: lo nishar echad: "not a single one remained." Total removal of the flies — complete, not partial. The comprehensive fulfillment of Moses's stated terms makes Pharaoh's subsequent hardening all the more stark: the proof was complete; the refusal that follows is therefore a fully informed rejection of YHWH's power.
32: vayakhbeid Paroh et-libbo gam bapa'am hazot: "Pharaoh hardened his heart even this time." Kaved (H3515) with Pharaoh as subject — the final such occurrence in the plague narrative. "Even this time" (gam bapa'am hazot) marks the cumulative character of each refusal: every pa'am (time, occasion) carries the weight of all previous occasions.
